nodes.name: Trivia Pavlovs
nodes.description: Common misconceptions and knee-jerk wrong answers — learn to second-guess your first instinct

items.prompt: The Sahara is the largest hot desert, but which landmass is the largest desert on Earth by total area?
items.explanation:
~14.2 million km² — nearly twice the Sahara's ~9.4 million km²
A desert is defined by precipitation (<250 mm/year), not temperature
The Arctic is the second-largest desert overall (~13.9 million km²)
Antarctica's coast receives only ~166 mm of precipitation per year; the interior gets far less
Contains ~70% of Earth's fresh water, locked in its ice sheet
The Sahara is third-largest overall, though first among hot deserts
items.cardFront: Largest desert on Earth by area?

items.prompt: Sydney is the largest city in Australia, but what is the actual national capital?
items.explanation:
Purpose-built as a compromise between rival cities Sydney and Melbourne
Constitution required the capital to be in New South Wales but at least 100 miles from Sydney
Selected in 1908, formally founded 1913; Parliament moved from Melbourne in 1927
Population ~450,000 — much smaller than Sydney (~5.3 million) or Melbourne (~5 million)
Designed by American architects Walter Burley Griffin and Marion Mahony Griffin, who won an international competition
Name likely derived from a local Ngunnawal word meaning 'meeting place'
items.cardFront: Capital of Australia?

items.prompt: Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone, but which Italian-American inventor demonstrated a voice-communication device years earlier and was later recognized by the U.S. House of Representatives?
items.explanation:
Demonstrated a voice-communication device ('telettrofono') as early as 1860
Filed a patent caveat in 1871 but couldn't afford the $10 renewal fee after 1874
U.S. House passed H.Res.269 on June 11, 2002, acknowledging his work in the invention of the telephone
Elisha Gray also filed a caveat the same day as Bell's patent (February 14, 1876)
Meucci was a Florentine immigrant who lived on Staten Island; he was impoverished for most of his American life
Bell's patent (No. 174,465) is sometimes called the most valuable patent ever issued
items.cardFront: Who demonstrated a telephone before Bell?

items.prompt: Thomas Edison patented the commercially practical incandescent bulb, but which English inventor demonstrated a working incandescent lamp before him?
items.explanation:
Demonstrated a working incandescent bulb in late 1878, before Edison's 1879 patent
Humphry Davy demonstrated an incandescent light using a platinum strip in 1802, decades earlier than either
Edison's key contribution was making the bulb commercially viable (better vacuum pump, longer-lasting carbonized filament)
Swan sued Edison in the UK for patent infringement and won; they merged their companies into 'Ediswan'
Swan's house in Gateshead was the first in the world lit by incandescent light (1880)
Swan also invented the dry photographic plate and an early synthetic fiber
items.cardFront: Who demonstrated an incandescent bulb before Edison?

items.prompt: Christopher Columbus reached the Caribbean in 1492, but which Norse explorer reached North America roughly 500 years earlier?
items.explanation:
Norse explorer who reached North America around 1000 CE
Archaeological site at L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland confirms Norse presence (tree-ring dating to 1021 CE)
Called the land 'Vinland' — likely Newfoundland or the Gulf of St. Lawrence region
Columbus reached Central America (Honduras, 1502) but never reached what is now the United States or Canada
Indigenous peoples had lived in the Americas for at least 15,000 years before either European arrival
Leif Erikson Day is observed in the U.S. on October 9
items.cardFront: Who reached North America ~500 years before Columbus?

items.prompt: Venus has the closest minimum approach to Earth, but which planet is closest to Earth on average over time?
items.explanation:
Mercury's tight orbit around the Sun means it is never very far from any planet
Venus has the closest minimum approach to Earth (~38 million km) but spends much of its orbit on the far side of the Sun
Mercury is the closest planet on average to every other planet in the solar system
Demonstrated in a 2019 Physics Today article using point-circle method calculations
Average Earth–Mercury distance: ~1.04 AU; average Earth–Venus distance: ~1.14 AU
Counterintuitive because 'closest orbit' and 'closest on average' are different questions
items.cardFront: Which planet is closest to Earth on average?

items.prompt: Many people believe Earth's distance from the Sun causes the seasons. What is the actual primary cause?
items.explanation:
Earth's 23.5-degree axial tilt causes seasons, not distance from the Sun
Earth is actually closest to the Sun (perihelion) in January, during Northern Hemisphere winter
Tilt affects both hours of sunlight per day and the angle at which sunlight strikes the surface
Distance from the Sun varies only ~3.4% between perihelion and aphelion — negligible effect
A planet with zero tilt (like Mercury) would have no seasons regardless of orbit shape
Uranus, with ~98° tilt, has the most extreme seasons in the solar system
items.cardFront: What actually causes Earth's seasons?

items.prompt: Aristotle listed five senses, but modern neurologists recognize many more. How many distinct senses do researchers routinely identify beyond the traditional five?
items.explanation:
Beyond sight, hearing, touch, taste, and smell: proprioception (body position), vestibular sense (balance), thermoception (temperature), nociception (pain)
Some researchers count 21–33 depending on classification criteria
The 'five senses' model comes from Aristotle's De Anima (~350 BCE)
Proprioception is sometimes called the 'sixth sense' — it lets you touch your nose with eyes closed
The brain consumes ~20% of the body's energy processing all sensory inputs
Interoception (awareness of internal states like hunger, thirst, heartbeat) is increasingly recognized as distinct
items.cardFront: How many senses do humans actually have?

items.prompt: The phrase 'Let them eat cake' is attributed to Marie Antoinette, but which philosopher actually wrote it decades before she arrived in France?
items.explanation:
Wrote 'Qu'ils mangent de la brioche' in his Confessions (Book 6, written c. 1765–1767) about 'a great princess'
Marie Antoinette was only about 10 years old at the time and had never been to France
'Brioche' is enriched bread, not cake — the English translation itself is misleading
The quote was first attributed to Marie Antoinette more than 50 years after the Revolution
Historian Antonia Fraser traces similar stories back to Maria Theresa, wife of Louis XIV
Rousseau's Confessions were published posthumously in 1782; the princess was never identified
items.cardFront: Who actually wrote 'Let them eat cake'?

items.prompt: Flight data recorders are universally called 'black boxes.' What color are they actually painted?
items.explanation:
Painted bright fluorescent orange (officially 'international orange') for visibility in wreckage
The name 'black box' likely originated from WWII-era RAF terminology for opaque electronic devices
Some theories trace the name to early fire-blackened prototypes after crash testing
Modern units contain both a flight data recorder and a cockpit voice recorder
Designed to withstand 3,400 g of impact force, 1,100°C for 60 minutes, and deep ocean pressure
Equipped with an underwater locator beacon ('pinger') that transmits for at least 30 days
items.cardFront: What color are aircraft 'black boxes'?

items.prompt: A popular myth claims Einstein failed math. What was his actual performance in math as a student?
items.explanation:
Einstein excelled at math — consistently top of his class in math and physics throughout primary school
Mastered differential and integral calculus before age 15
He failed the ETH Zurich entrance exam overall at age 16, but scored highest in math and physics; he failed only non-scientific subjects
He was two years younger than typical applicants for that exam
The myth may stem from confusion between grading systems: in Switzerland 6 is the best grade, but in Germany 1 is the best and 6 is the worst
Later collaborated with mathematician Marcel Grossmann on the tensor calculus underlying general relativity
items.cardFront: Did Einstein fail math?

items.prompt: Chameleons are famous for changing color, but their primary reason is not camouflage. What is the main reason they change color?
items.explanation:
Primary reasons: dominance displays, mating signals, aggression, and thermoregulation
Males achieving brighter coloration are more likely to win territorial contests without physical fighting
As ectotherms, they darken to absorb heat and lighten to cool down
Color change uses tunable nanocrystal lattices in iridophore cells, not pigment redistribution
Camouflage is a secondary benefit, not the primary driver of color change
A 2015 Nature Communications paper revealed the nanocrystal mechanism, overturning the pigment theory
items.cardFront: Why do chameleons actually change color?

items.prompt: Medieval cathedral windows are often thicker at the bottom. What manufacturing technique actually caused this uneven thickness?
items.explanation:
Glass is an amorphous solid, not a supercooled liquid — it does not flow at room temperature
At room temperature, glass would take ~10³² years to flow a measurable amount
In the crown glass method, molten glass was blown into a bubble, then spun into a flat disc
Panes cut from the disc were naturally thicker near the edges and thinner toward the center
Glaziers typically installed the heavier edge at the bottom for stability
The 'glass is a liquid' myth persists in many textbooks despite being thoroughly debunked
items.cardFront: Why are medieval windows thicker at the bottom?

items.prompt: Bulls charge at a matador's cape, but they cannot actually see the color red. What are they reacting to instead?
items.explanation:
Bulls are dichromats — they see blues and yellows but cannot perceive red
MythBusters tested: stationary flags of any color got weak reactions; waving flags triggered charges regardless of color
The muleta (cape) is red to mask bloodstains for the audience's benefit, not to provoke the bull
Bulls are also agitated by the overall stressful situation (crowds, noise, picadors wounding them)
The smaller red muleta is used only in the final third of the fight; earlier passes use a larger magenta-and-yellow capote
Calves raised in complete isolation still charge at moving objects, confirming the instinct is motion-based
items.cardFront: What makes bulls charge at the cape?

items.prompt: The only complete Viking-era helmet ever found is the Gjermundbu helmet from 10th-century Norway. What does it look like?
items.explanation:
The Gjermundbu helmet is a rounded iron cap with a spectacle-shaped eye and nose guard — no horns
The famous Viksø horned helmets are from the Nordic Bronze Age (~900 BCE), over 1,000 years before the Vikings
The horned-helmet myth was popularized by 19th-century Romantic artists and Wagner's Ring Cycle opera costumes (1876)
Horns would have been impractical in combat — they catch on shields, branches, and ship rigging
Norse sagas never describe horned helmets; they mention plain metal or leather headgear
The image became cemented by Swedish artist August Malmström's illustrations for Frithiof's Saga in the 1880s
items.cardFront: What did Viking helmets actually look like?

items.prompt: A common myth says humans use only 10% of their brain. What percentage do brain imaging studies show is actually active?
items.explanation:
Brain imaging (fMRI, PET) shows all regions are active to some degree, even during sleep
The brain consumes ~20% of the body's energy despite being only ~2% of body mass
Damage to almost any brain area causes measurable deficits — no region is expendable
The myth may trace to William James's 1907 statement about untapped human 'potential' (not anatomy)
Popularized by films like Lucy (2014) and Limitless (2011), which treat it as scientific fact
Neurologist Barry Gordon called the myth 'so wrong it is almost laughable'
items.cardFront: What percentage of the brain do we use?

items.prompt: Goldfish are said to have a 3-second memory. Research shows they can actually retain learned behaviors for how long?
items.explanation:
Research dating to the 1960s shows goldfish can remember things for at least five months
They can learn to associate specific times of day with feeding and anticipate it
They can be trained to push colored paddles for food and retain that skill weeks later
A 15-year-old Australian student's 2008 science project replicated the result and made international news
Goldfish can also navigate mazes and recognize their owners
The 3-second myth has no known scientific origin and may come from a 1990s advertising campaign
items.cardFront: How long is a goldfish's memory?

items.prompt: During the 1692 Salem witch trials, how were the convicted 'witches' actually executed?
items.explanation:
19 people were hanged (14 women, 5 men) between June and September 1692
Giles Corey was pressed to death with heavy stones for refusing to enter a plea — the only such death in Massachusetts history
At least five more accused died in jail while awaiting trial
English common law only permitted burning for high treason or heresy, not witchcraft
The confusion comes from Continental European witch trials, where burning at the stake was common
Governor William Phips dissolved the special court in October 1692 after his own wife was accused
items.cardFront: How were Salem 'witches' executed?

items.prompt: Parents often blame sugar for making children hyperactive. What have controlled scientific studies consistently found?
items.explanation:
Multiple double-blind studies and a major 1995 JAMA meta-analysis found no causal connection between sugar and hyperactivity
The NIH stated as early as 1982 that no connection had been scientifically proven
Parents who believe their child consumed sugar rate the child as more hyperactive regardless — textbook expectation bias
The myth traces to allergist Benjamin Feingold's 1973 AMA conference presentation about food additives (not sugar) and behavior
The context where sugar is consumed (parties, holidays, excitement) explains the perceived effect
Sugar does cause a brief blood glucose spike, but this does not translate to behavioral changes in controlled settings
items.cardFront: Does sugar cause hyperactivity in children?

items.prompt: Napoleon is famously described as very short. What was his actual height in modern measurements?
items.explanation:
~170 cm (5'7") — average or slightly above average for a Frenchman of his era (average ~5'5")
His French measurement of '5 pieds 2 pouces' was misread using English inches instead of French pouces
French pouces were longer than English inches (2.7 cm vs. 2.54 cm), inflating the conversion error
British propaganda cartoonist James Gillray deliberately depicted him as tiny starting in 1803
His nickname 'le petit caporal' was a term of affection, not a comment on his height
His tall Imperial Guard (minimum height ~5'10") may have made him appear shorter by comparison
items.cardFront: How tall was Napoleon actually?

items.prompt: Sherlock Holmes is famous for saying 'Elementary, my dear Watson.' In which Arthur Conan Doyle story does he actually say this exact phrase?
items.explanation:
The exact phrase never appears in any of the 60 Sherlock Holmes stories by Conan Doyle
Holmes says 'Elementary' and 'my dear Watson' separately at various points but never combined
The combined quote first appeared in P.G. Wodehouse's Psmith, Journalist (1915)
Cemented in popular culture by the Basil Rathbone/Nigel Bruce film series (1939–1946)
Similar misquotes: 'Play it again, Sam' (never said in Casablanca), 'Beam me up, Scotty' (never said in Star Trek)
The closest Doyle gets is in 'The Crooked Man' (1893): Watson says 'Excellent!' and Holmes replies 'Elementary'
items.cardFront: In which story does Holmes say this exact phrase?

items.prompt: Michelangelo spent four years painting the Sistine Chapel ceiling. In what position did he actually work?
items.explanation:
Stood on specially designed scaffolding, craning his neck upward with arms raised overhead
Drew a self-portrait sketch showing himself painting in this standing position with a humorous poem about the pain
Scaffolding projected from brackets set high in the wall, about 20 meters above the floor
The 'lying on his back' myth was popularized by the 1965 film The Agony and the Ecstasy starring Charlton Heston
Michelangelo suffered severe back and neck pain and chronic eye strain from paint dripping into his eyes
The project took 1508–1512; he painted ~465 m² of ceiling, mostly without assistants for the figures
items.cardFront: How did Michelangelo paint the Sistine ceiling?

items.prompt: Robert Burns is credited with writing 'Auld Lang Syne,' but what did he actually do with the song?
items.explanation:
Burns sent the lyrics to Mrs. Frances Dunlop in a 1788 letter, describing them as transcribed 'from an old man's singing'
Sir Robert Ayton published 'Old Long Syne' in 1711; Allan Ramsay published a version in 1720
Burns likely wrote at least two verses himself but adapted the rest from folk tradition
The melody commonly used today comes from a tune in William Shield's 1782 opera Rosina
Burns's version was published posthumously in 1796 in James Johnson's Scots Musical Museum
The song became a New Year's Eve tradition largely through Guy Lombardo's annual broadcasts starting in 1929
items.cardFront: What did Burns actually do with 'Auld Lang Syne'?

items.prompt: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin mould in 1928, but which two scientists developed it into a usable medicine?
items.explanation:
Fleming lacked the chemistry expertise and resources to turn mould observation into a drug
Florey and Chain at Oxford developed methods for cultivating, extracting, purifying, and mass-producing penicillin starting in 1939
All three shared the 1945 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine
Fleming himself described his sole-credit fame as the 'Fleming Myth'
U.S. pharmaceutical companies (Pfizer, Merck, Squibb) scaled production for WWII using deep-tank fermentation
First clinical use was on Albert Alexander in 1941; he initially improved but died when the supply ran out
items.cardFront: Who turned penicillin into a usable drug?

items.prompt: A common claim says the Great Wall of China is visible from space. China's first astronaut Yang Liwei looked for it in 2003. What did he report?
items.explanation:
Yang Liwei confirmed in 2003 he could not see the Great Wall from orbit
The wall is long (~21,000 km including branches) but only ~10 meters wide, with low contrast against surrounding terrain
Other man-made structures (highways, airports, cities at night) are far more visible from orbit
The myth dates to at least a 1932 Ripley's Believe It or Not cartoon
One ambiguous photo was taken from the ISS by astronaut Leroy Chiao in 2004, but even he was not sure what he saw
NASA has stated the wall is generally not visible to the unaided eye from low Earth orbit
items.cardFront: Can you see the Great Wall from space?
